companyCaseStudy.title: How Discord Scaled Real-Time Chat & Voice to 6.7M Concurrent Users (Gateway, Shards, and BEAM magic)
companyCaseStudy.summary: Discord needed to support millions of concurrent WebSocket connections and realtime voice sessions (peak 6.7M concurrent users) without exploding cost or latency. They re-architected the gateway using the BEAM (Elixir/Erlang) model, sharded connections, and an event bus to achieve massive concurrency, predictable latency, and far lower memory per connection.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

In January 2021 Discord announced a peak of ~6.7 million concurrent users and ~140 million monthly active users — that’s an enormous scale for persistent WebSocket + realtime voice traffic. Traditional thread/process models and heavyweight socket stacks couldn't handle the scale economically. The challenge: keep connection memory low, keep fan-out latency tight, and avoid cascading failures when traffic spikes.

---

## Problem

- Millions of persistent WebSocket connections and tens of thousands of concurrent voice sessions.
- Heavy fan-out: a message in a large public channel can need to reach hundreds of thousands of connected clients.
- Early gateway stacks (Python/Node-like approaches) used too much memory per connection and made graceful failure and fast restarts difficult.
- Need: low-latency message fan-out, efficient presence, and voice/media handling without requiring thousands of boxes.

## Challenge

- Reduce cost by minimizing memory/CPU per connection.
- Make the gateway horizontally scalable and resilient to software restarts/rollouts.
- Provide sub-100ms fan-out latency for typing/presence and predictable performance for voice sessions.
- Integrate with existing microservices (auth, routing, storage) without adding coupling.

## Solution (The Story)

Discord made several concrete architectural decisions:

1. Move the gateway to BEAM (Elixir/Erlang) to exploit extremely lightweight processes (one process per connection), preemptive scheduling, and fast failure/restart semantics.
2. Shard the gateway: each gateway instance owns a subset of "shards" (groups of client connections) to localize state and reduce cross-node coordination.
3. Use a high-throughput event bus for service-to-service events and for cross-shard fan-out (Discord uses a message bus / pub-sub layer between gateways and services).
4. Keep ephemeral state (presence, ephemeral caches) in an in-memory distributed store (Redis-like) and durable metadata in RDBMS/strong store.
5. Separate voice/media path from control: signaling over the gateway, media via UDP media servers and WebRTC/STUN/TURN.

These choices let Discord carry millions of persistent sockets with predictable resource usage and fast failure recovery.

### Why BEAM (Elixir/Erlang)?

- BEAM processes are tiny (~a few KB of memory max per process depending on workload) vs threaded models which often cost hundreds of KB per socket.
- Built-in supervisor trees make failures localized and automatic restarts easy.
- Soft real-time scheduling ensures a misbehaving connection doesn't starve others.

### Architectural overview

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[User Client (Web/Native)] -->|WS / Gateway API| Gateway[Gateway Cluster (Elixir BEAM)]
  Gateway -->|Publish Events| MsgBus[Event Bus (Kafka/Rabbit/Kinesis)]
  MsgBus --> Micro[Microservices (Go/TS) - business logic]
  Gateway -->|Presence ops| Presence[Redis Cluster / Ephemeral Store]
  Micro -->|Persist| DB[(Postgres / Durable DB)]
  Client -->|Voice Signaling| Gateway
  Gateway -->|Signaling| Voice[Media Servers / WebRTC / TURN]
  Voice -->|RTP / UDP| Client
```

### The flow for a chat message (simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  C[Client sends message] --> G[Gateway instance]
  G --> B[Publish to Event Bus]
  B --> S[Shard gateways that subscribe]
  S --> C2[Deliver to connected clients]
```

## Before vs After (high-level numbers)

| Metric | Before (legacy stack) | After (Elixir + sharded gateways) |
|--------|-----------------------:|----------------------------------:|
| Peak concurrent users supported per cluster | ~hundreds of thousands | millions (Discord reported 6.7M global peak) |
| Memory per connection (approx.) | 100s KB → 1+ MB | 10s KB → few 10s KB |
| Required gateway hosts for same load | Thousands | Hundreds (order-of-magnitude reduction) |
| Fan-out latency (edge-to-client) | 100s ms — seconds (under load) | sub-100ms typical |

> Note: the numbers above reflect the *order-of-magnitude* improvements that motivated the migration (public Discord engineering posts highlight the scalability gains after adopting BEAM/gateway sharding).

## Key implementation details & trade-offs

- Sharding: Discord shards by logical ranges (shard ID) so that a given gateway only tracks a subset of connections and local fan-out is O(local connections) instead of global. Cross-shard messages go through the event bus.
- Event bus: lightweight, horizontally scalable pub/sub (Kafka/RabbitMQ style) for cross-gateway events and to decouple gateways from microservices.
- Presence: presence information is kept in an ephemeral fast store (Redis cluster or similar) and replicated / published so clients see who’s online quickly.
- Voice/media: control (signaling) stays on the gateway, but RTP flows go peer-to-peer or via specialized media servers to offload CPU and bandwidth.
- Observability: extensive metrics and health checks on per-shard and per-node level; use of supervisor trees for fast restarts and avoiding noisy neighbor impacts.

## Results

- Supported a global peak of ~6.7M concurrent users while keeping costs and hosts at manageable levels (Discord publicly called out that the architecture enables them to scale without prohibitive hardware growth).
- Dramatic reduction in memory footprint per connection, enabling fewer hosts for the same number of sockets.
- Predictable restart behavior: gateway nodes can be rolled with minimal connection churn; BEAM supervisors keep runtime stable.
- Sub-100ms fan-out latency in many cases; voice sessions routed to efficient media servers improved audio latency and reliability.

## Visual comparison (Before vs After)

| Dimension | Legacy Gateway (Python/Node) | BEAM-based Gateway (Elixir/Erlang) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Concurrency model | Thread / async-per-connection (heavier) | Lightweight processes per connection (BEAM) |
| Failure recovery | Process-level crashes can be noisy | Supervisor trees enable localized restarts |
| Memory per socket | High (100s KB – MB) | Low (10s KB) |
| Fan-out approach | Synchronous broadcast or heavy central coordination | Localized shard fan-out + event bus for cross-shard |

## Lessons & Practical advice

- If you need millions of persistent connections, consider the per-connection memory footprint of your runtime. The BEAM is specifically optimized for many small isolated processes.
- Localize state via sharding to reduce coordination costs and make failure domains small.
- Decouple via a message/event bus — it allows multi-language microservices to subscribe and keeps gateways simple.
- Separate control and media: keep signaling light on the gateway and offload media to specialized servers or peer connections.

---

## One-sentence summary

By switching the gateway to a BEAM-based architecture, sharding connections, and adding a scalable event bus + ephemeral presence store, Discord handled multi-million concurrent connections, kept fan-out latency low, and reduced host count dramatically.
keyTakeaways: Use a runtime with tiny per-connection overhead (BEAM/Elixir) to support millions of concurrent WebSockets without exploding host counts.
Shard connection state to localize fan-out and failure domains; use an event bus for cross-shard coordination.
Separate signaling from media: keep the gateway lightweight and offload RTP/voice to specialized media servers or peer connections.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
defmodule SimpleGateway do
  use GenServer

  # Each client connection is represented by a lightweight BEAM process.
  # This pseudocode shows a tiny supervision model for connections.

  def start_link(_opts) do
    Supervisor.start_link([
      {Registry, keys: :unique, name: SimpleGateway.Registry},
      {DynamicSupervisor, strategy: :one_for_one, name: SimpleGateway.ConnSup}
    ], strategy: :one_for_one)
  end

  def accept_socket(socket) do
    DynamicSupervisor.start_child(SimpleGateway.ConnSup, {ConnectionWorker, socket})
  end

  defmodule ConnectionWorker do
    use GenServer

    def start_link(socket), do: GenServer.start_link(__MODULE__, socket)

    def init(socket) do
      # subscribe to shard-local pubsub, register presence, etc.
      :ok = Phoenix.PubSub.subscribe(:local_pubsub, make_ref())
      {:ok, %{socket: socket}}
    end

    def handle_info({:deliver, payload}, state) do
      # push payload down the websocket efficiently
      :ok = :cowboy_websocket.send(state.socket, {:text, payload})
      {:noreply, state}
    end
  end
end
furtherReading: https://discord.com/blog/discords-growth-and-community-updates (search Discord blog for concurrent user announcements)

concept.title: Split the Traffic Smartly: Practical Load Balancing Algorithms Every Developer Should Know
concept.summary: Load balancers sit between users and your servers — picking the right algorithm prevents hotspots, reduces latency, and keeps capacity predictable. This guide shows common algorithms, when to choose each, practical configs, and pitfalls to avoid.
concept.explanation:
## Why Load Balancing Matters

Imagine a busy restaurant with one host seating guests. If the host sends everyone to the first empty table, that table gets overwhelmed while others sit idle. A good host spreads guests so service stays smooth. A load balancer does the same for incoming requests across servers.

Load balancing is about fairness, utilization, and latency. The algorithm your load balancer uses affects request distribution, tail latency, and how well you utilize instances under variable traffic.

### Analogy

- Round robin = dealing cards evenly to players.
- Least connections = sending new customers to the waiter with the fewest current tables.
- Power of Two Choices = pick two random waiters, send the customer to the less busy one — cheap and effective.


### How It Works (Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client Request] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB -->|Select algorithm| Selector[Choose backend]
  Selector --> B1[Backend A]
  Selector --> B2[Backend B]
  Selector --> B3[Backend C]
  B1 --> DB[(Database)]
  B2 --> DB
  B3 --> DB
```

### Example Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>LB: HTTP Request
  LB->>Monitor: Check backend health & metrics
  alt backend healthy
    LB->>Server1: Forward request
    Server1-->>Client: Response
  else backend unhealthy
    LB->>Server2: Forward request
    Server2-->>Client: Response
  end
```

### Common Algorithms (what they do, pros/cons, when to use)

| Algorithm | How it chooses | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|----------------|------|------|------------|
| Round Robin | Cycle through the pool | Simple, fair for equal servers | Ignores load/capacity, poor for heterogeneous servers | Small clusters with similar servers |
| Weighted Round Robin | Like RR but weights servers | Accounts for different capacities | Need good weight tuning | Mixed VM sizes or mixed container resources |
| Least Connections | Picks server with fewest active connections | Good for long-lived connections (WebSocket, streaming) | Needs accurate connection tracking | Real-time services, long connections |
| Least Response Time | Picks lowest observed response time | Lowers latency tail, reacts to slowdown | Requires metrics/monitoring | Latency-sensitive services with fresh metrics |
| IP Hash / Source Affinity | Hash client IP to server | Sticky by client without cookies | Uneven distribution if user IPs skewed | Simple stickiness needed, small deployments |
| Sticky Sessions (cookies) | Binder by cookie/session token | Works for stateful apps | Ties client to server; limits scaling and complicates restarts | Legacy stateful apps (temporary) |
| Power of Two Choices | Pick two random servers, choose the less loaded | Very low overhead, excellent balance | Needs simple load metric (active reqs) | Large pools where monitoring every server is expensive |
| Random | Uniform random pick | Extremely simple, surprisingly ok at scale | Variance may cause short-lived hotspots | Massive pools, stateless workloads |


### Before / After: What a smarter algorithm changes

| Scenario | Before (DNS RR) | After (LB with least-response-time + health checks) |
|---------|------------------|----------------------------------------------------|
| Avg latency | 120ms | 85ms |
| Peak load handling | Overwhelmed single node | Smooth degradation, reduced queueing |
| Failed node handling | Some clients still reach dead node | Fast failover via health checks |


### Practical examples

Nginx upstream simple weighted example:

```nginx
upstream backend {
  server 10.0.0.1:8080 weight=5;
  server 10.0.0.2:8080 weight=2;
  server 10.0.0.3:8080 weight=1;
}

server {
  listen 80;
  location / {
    proxy_pass http://backend;
  }
}
```

Power-of-two-choices (pseudo-JS) for selecting backend by active requests:

```js
const backends = [
  { url: 'http://10.0.0.1', active: 0 },
  { url: 'http://10.0.0.2', active: 0 },
  { url: 'http://10.0.0.3', active: 0 }
];

function pick() {
  const a = Math.floor(Math.random() * backends.length);
  let b = Math.floor(Math.random() * backends.length);
  while (b === a && backends.length > 1) b = Math.floor(Math.random() * backends.length);
  return backends[ backends[a].active <= backends[b].active ? a : b ];
}

// On request: const chosen = pick(); chosen.active++; proxyTo(chosen); chosen.active-- on finish
```

### When to change algorithms

- Start with round robin or a managed cloud LB default for simple stateless services.
- Switch to least-connections for long-lived connections (chat, websockets).
- Use weighted algorithms when instance sizes differ.
- Use power-of-two-choices in very large pools where polling global metrics is expensive.
- Combine sticky sessions only as a last resort; prefer external session stores.

### Common Pitfalls

- Treating load balancer as a database: keep LBs stateless and replaceable.
- Sticky sessions hiding scaling problems: you may mask uneven capacity and complicate failover.
- Ignoring health checks: a bad node must be removed quickly to avoid wasted traffic.
- Using DNS RR without short TTLs: changes propagate slowly and can keep hitting dead backends.
- Time-varying load and long tail: average metrics hide spikes — monitor percentiles (p95/p99).

### Quick wins

- Add active health checks (HTTP /health) and remove unhealthy backends fast.
- Expose per-backend metrics (active requests, response time) to the LB.
- Use weighted algorithms when VMs or containers are heterogeneous.
- Prefer session store (Redis) over sticky sessions for scaling.

### Wrap-up

Choosing the right algorithm reduces latency, prevents hotspots, and improves reliability. Start simple, measure p95/p99, and evolve the algorithm as traffic patterns and workload characteristics become clearer.
keyTakeaways: Pick the algorithm to match workload: least-connections for long-lived sessions, weighted for heterogeneous capacity, power-of-two-choices for large pools.
Always combine an algorithm with health checks and metrics (active requests, response time) to avoid sending traffic to bad nodes.
Avoid sticky sessions where possible; externalize session state to scale and recover gracefully.
